# Introduction & the inferential mismatch {#sec-introduction}

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Guardrails honored in this chapter

- **G-24** — The calibrated-Bayes principle (judge a Bayesian model against
  frequentist operating characteristics; preserve the inference center while
  realigning covariance) is established prior art in Little (2012) and Rubin (1984);
  only its application to the meta-analytic RVE/CHE sandwich target is this review's
  contribution.
- **G-23** — The "never joined" claim is a *documented systematic absence*, phrased
  conditionally as an unoccupied conjunction; it is defended downstream, not proved
  here.
- **G-27** — "Never joined" means the *conjunction* of an RVE/CHE-sandwich target and
  a Little/Rubin license is unoccupied; it is never a priority claim over any single
  ingredient (posterior recoloring, sandwich estimation, or the calibrated-Bayes
  principle), each of which exists in print.
:::

Defining a Bayesian robust variance estimator for dependent-effect meta-analysis is
the work of three literatures, not one. This chapter names the three literatures, the
join this review proposes between them, and the roadmap of the funnel the
rest of the book descends. The object the funnel tracks — the marginal RVE/CHE
sandwich covariance — is introduced here and never leaves: every chapter hands the
same target to the next.

## 1.1 The three-literatures mismatch {#sec-three-literatures}

The problem this book addresses is an inferential mismatch. Defining such an estimator
is the work of three literatures rather than one, each supplying one necessary but
individually insufficient component [([CL-P0-01](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p0-01), HEURISTIC; G-24)]{.claim-tag}. Naming the mismatch first, rather than any one
field, matters because the contribution is a join across fields rather than an
improvement within one, and the join is invisible until all three components are named
together.

The first component is **robust cluster-sandwich estimation**, originating with
@hedges2010. This lineage fixes the *target*: it points a cluster-robust sandwich at
the marginal covariance of a dependent effect-size vector through a working model
(aggregated or correlated-effects) that may misspecify the within-study correlation,
and it secures consistency in the number of studies without requiring the true
within-study correlation to be known. The marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance is the
object this literature owns, and the object the other two literatures will be
asked to host. What this literature does not supply is a posterior: it furnishes a
covariance target and an estimator for it, not a Bayesian inferential machine.

The second component is the **Bayesian hierarchical and multilevel meta-analysis**
apparatus. This tradition supplies the *modeling machinery*: weakly informative
heterogeneity priors, observation-level random effects, multivariate and three-level
structures, and the posterior as the primary inferential object. Its strength is
expressiveness: it can represent within- and between-study dependence hierarchically
and preserve observation-level design matrices. What it does not, on its own, supply
is a *named covariance target*. Because the Bayesian analyst keeps the posterior as
the primary object, a frequentist sandwich covariance is not a standard reporting
quantity in this literature; the modeling apparatus is present, but the target the
first literature owns is not among its objectives.

The third component is the **calibrated-Bayes philosophy** of @little2012 and
@rubin1984. Here a Bayesian model is judged against frequentist operating
characteristics, and the governing move is to preserve the inference center while
realigning the covariance toward a frequentist operating-characteristic target. These
two works establish the principle as standing prior art. This literature supplies the
*license* (the warrant for importing a frequentist covariance target into Bayesian
inference), but it is a general principle rather than a meta-analytic method, and it
points at no particular target and no particular application. Crediting the principle to
Little and Rubin is a binding discipline (G-24): this review invents neither
calibrated Bayes nor the idea of preserving the center while realigning covariance;
only the application of that principle to the meta-analytic sandwich target is novel
here.

Each component is necessary, and none suffices alone. The first owns a covariance
target but offers no posterior; the second owns a posterior but names no covariance
target; the third owns the license that would authorize importing the one into the
other, yet it is prior art that has not been aimed at the target. The mismatch is
therefore structural rather than a matter of any field's deficiency: three mature
literatures supply three parts of a single inferential object, and that object is
well-defined only when the parts are assembled at the point where they meet, the
covariance.

## 1.2 The join this review proposes {#sec-the-join}

This review's move is to perform that assembly explicitly, carrying the marginal
RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the object held across all three literatures. The
frequentist RVE lineage, from @hedges2010 through @pustejovsky2024, made the robust
cluster-sandwich the structural standard for dependent-effect meta-analysis, fixing
the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the target any analogue would have to
match; calibrated-Bayes theory [@little2012; @rubin1984] supplies the
intellectual license to import that same sandwich covariance into Bayesian inference
as an explicit calibration target [([CL-P0-02](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p0-02), POSITIONING; G-23, G-24, G-27)]{.claim-tag}. The two
streams are real and independently established: the sandwich-as-standard clause is
anchored at its origin in Hedges, Tipton and Johnson (2010) and capped by Pustejovsky
and Chen (2024), while the license clause rests on Little and Rubin in the survey and
official-statistics context where those authors worked. Stated as a movement of one
object, the proposal is to take the covariance target the frequentist lineage
already owns and, under the calibrated-Bayes license, adopt it as the object a
Bayesian posterior is calibrated to match, preserving the posterior mean while
realigning its covariance toward that target.

This conjunction has not been occupied, and the claim that it has not is made here
conditionally and defended downstream. Across a broad survey of Bayesian
meta-analysis, these three literatures have not been joined at the relevant point:
no published meta-analysis has pointed the calibrated-Bayes principle at the marginal
RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target. The wording governs
the whole book. The conjunction is *unoccupied*, not
*unattainable*: each ingredient already exists in print (posterior recoloring toward
some sandwich, robust sandwich estimation, and the calibrated-Bayes principle have
each been used somewhere), and this review claims no priority over any single one
(G-27). Both qualifiers are retained, the RVE/CHE sandwich *target* and the
Little/Rubin *license*, so that near-neighbors which recolor toward a different
sandwich under a different license are not miscounted as occupants. The assertion
rests on a documented systematic search, its strength equals the breadth of that
search, and it is never escalated to a proof that no such method could exist (G-23).
The verbatim negative-existence statement is therefore deferred: it is asserted once,
with its full search breadth, where the Bayesian gap is established (Chapter 3), and
restated as positioning where the contribution is assembled (Chapter 6). Here the
claim is only that the conjunction is open and that this review proposes to occupy it.

The absence admits a non-pejorative reading. It reflects no deficiency in the Bayesian
apparatus, only that a posterior calibrated to a marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance
has not been a standard object in this literature. What is missing is not Bayesian
capacity but the application of an established license to an established target. The
principle that licenses the occupation remains Little's and Rubin's; the survey-to-Bayes
mechanism this review later adapts remains Williams and Savitsky's; what is new is the
application: pointing an established principle, through an adapted mechanism, at a
target the frequentist literature already owns (G-24).

## 1.3 Roadmap of the funnel {#sec-roadmap}

The rest of the book is one descent, tracking the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance
through the three literatures in four beats.

- **P1 — RVE owns it (Ch. 2).** The frequentist RVE lineage fixed the marginal
  sandwich covariance as the estimand, disciplined the small-sample regime with named
  corrections, and shipped the canonical software. This chapter establishes the target
  the rest of the funnel must match.
- **P2 — Bayesian meta-analysis lacks it (Ch. 3).** The Bayesian tradition arrives at
  that target with ample modeling capacity and no analogue. This chapter documents the
  gap (an absence of *application*, not of Bayesian capacity) and hosts the verbatim
  gap statement.
- **P3 — calibrated-Bayes permits it (Ch. 4).** The calibrated-Bayes principle supplies
  the missing license, the Williams–Savitsky survey-to-Bayes Cholesky mechanism
  supplies the operation, and this review names the result CCC. This chapter shows the
  join is permitted and how it is built.
- **P4 — CCC delivers it, with diagnostics (Ch. 5).** A specifically Bayesian RVE earns
  its place through capabilities the frequentist sandwich does not readily afford
  (flexible heterogeneity priors, observation-level structure, and per-coefficient
  design-effect-ratio diagnostics), with covariance-matching and coverage held strictly
  separate.

The two unoccupied conjunctions then close affirmatively in **Ch. 6**, where the gap,
this review's matching results, and the matching-versus-coverage distinction are
assembled into the stated contribution and the claim-type discipline is set out as a
reading key; **Ch. 7** bounds the contribution, framing its scope as preconditions
rather than apologies. The first beat begins now: the next chapter develops only the
first conjunct, namely what that owned frequentist standard is and what covariance it
targets.
